title: Hankel low-rank matrix approximation for gravitational-wave data analysis
comment: 17 pages, 5 figures; for associated implementation, see https://github.com/CosmoVlad/lisa-hankel/tree/main/pipelines
summary: Next-generation gravitational-wave (GW) detectors, such as the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA), will observe vast numbers of overlapping signals. Disentangling these signals from instrumental noise and from one another constitutes a significant data analysis challenge. We explore a denoising technique based on embedding time series into Hankel matrices: a superposition of $n$ (damped) sinusoids corresponds to a matrix of rank $2n$. Thus, the problem of signal extraction is reduced to a structured low-rank approximation problem. Using synthetic data tailored to GW applications, we benchmark three Hankel-based algorithms: ESPRIT, Cadzow iterations, and iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS). Our test scenarios include isolated and multi-component monochromatic signals, the resolution of sources with closely spaced frequencies, and the recovery of black hole quasinormal modes (QNM). All three algorithms achieve near-optimal performance consistent with Fisher matrix bounds, evidenced by an inverse-square scaling of the mismatch with the signal-to-noise ratio. Furthermore, a proof-of-concept application to numerical relativity waveforms validates the ability of these algorithms to extract QNM frequencies from ringdown signals. Hankel low-rank approximation therefore offers a transparent, computationally efficient avenue for preprocessing GW time series.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using Hankel matrix-based low-rank approximation techniques to disentangle overlapping gravitational-wave signals from noise in future detectors like LISA, demonstrating near-optimal performance with three algorithms.
AI.motivation: Next-gen GW detectors will face challenges with overlapping signals and noise, requiring innovative denoising methods to accurately extract signals.
AI.method: Embeds time series into Hankel matrices where sinusoidal signals create low-rank structures. Tests ESPRIT, Cadzow iterations, and IRLS algorithms on synthetic and numerical relativity data.
AI.result: All algorithms achieve performance matching Fisher matrix bounds, showing inverse-square scaling of mismatch with SNR, and successfully recover QNM frequencies from ringdown signals.
AI.conclusion: Hankel-based methods provide an efficient, transparent preprocessing approach for GW data, effective for multi-component signals and closely spaced frequencies.

title: BL Lac host galaxies: how to systematically characterise them in optical-NIR spectroscopy
summary: Host galaxies of Active Galactic Nuclei give crucial information on the interaction between accreting Supermassive Black Holes and their surroundings, and on their common evolution. Their study in the case of aligned jetted AGN - BL Lacertae objects in particular - is complicated by the non-thermal jet component, whose bright and multi-frequency emission easily dominates over the whole electromagnetic spectrum. BL Lac host galaxies have thus been sparsely studied, and their elliptical nature is currently a hypothesis supported by few observations. With the broad aim of a systematic analysis of these sources, and in light of the many optical and NIR spectroscopic facilities that are now available, we implement an easily applicable method to determine whether a BL Lac is hosted in an elliptical or spiral galaxy. Building on the only systematic study currently available on BL Lac hosts, we worked on a sample of realistic BL Lac synthetic spectra. We analysed them and characterized their statistics using QSFit, a publicly available spectroscopy software. If BL Lac host galaxies were both elliptical and spiral, our method would be able to discriminate between them, provided that BL Lac jets are fainter than $L_γ\sim10^{46}$erg/s. Just two runs of QSFit for each BL Lac spectrum would return a single parameter, that would allow for a first broad distinction between the two classes. We finally discuss the two galaxy types that introduce some uncertainty in their classification, that might lead to possible classification biases.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to classify BL Lac hosted galaxies as elliptical or spiral using optical/NIR spectral analysis with QSFit software, demonstrating it works if their gamma-ray luminosity is below ~1e46 erg/s.
AI.motivation: BL Lac host galaxies are poorly studied due to overpowering jet emission, making it hard to determine their galaxy type (elliptical vs spiral). Existing evidence only tentatively supports them being elliptical. A reliable classification method is needed to understand AGN-galaxy interactions and coevolution.
AI.method: Using synthetic BL Lac spectra from prior systematic studies, the method applies two QSFit runs per spectrum to derive a discriminant parameter. QSFit decomposes spectral features to identify host galaxy type despite jet contamination. Analysis includes statistical characterization of classification effectiveness.
AI.result: The method can区分elliptical/spiral hosts when gamma-ray luminosity <1e46 erg/s, allowing preliminary classification. However, certain galaxy subclasses may cause classification uncertainties and potential biases.
AI.conclusion: QSFit-based spectral analysis offers a viable pathway for large-scale BL Lac host classification, though limitations exist at high jet luminosities and require caution with ambiguous cases. This sets foundation for future systematic surveys using new optical/NIR facilities.

title: Single-source-class interpretation of the diffuse astrophysical neutrino flux
summary: We explore the interpretation that the diffuse astrophysical neutrino flux is dominated by a single standard candle-like source class. Since recent observations favor a broken power law with a spectral break around 30 TeV, we postulate that the $pγ$ channel is the dominant neutrino production process creating a peak at these energies. We use a SOPHIA-based photo-pion interaction model with a thermal target including high-energy processes, such as multi-pion production, which turns out to be relevant for the interpretation. We demonstrate that target photon temperatures 0.1 to 1 keV are preferred in a multi-parameter fit, whereas the maximal neutrino energies can be limited by A) soft injection spectra, B) a maximal proton energy in the PeV range, or C) magnetic field effects on the secondary muons, pions, and kaons with B in the few 10 kG range. We predict that future measurements, such as of the neutrino flavor composition or neutrino-antineutrino ratio (Glashow resonance), can discriminate scenarios. We also point out that the parameters obtained in our generic approach, such as in the strong magnetic field values, might be indicative for an AGN core origin as a driver of the diffuse flux.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether a single standard candle-like source class dominates the diffuse astrophysical neutrino flux, proposing the $pγ$ channel as the dominant neutrino production mechanism. It uses a SOPHIA-based photo-pion model with thermal target photons and finds that target photon temperatures between 0.1-1 keV and specific energy-limiting factors (like soft spectra, PeV proton energy caps, or magnetic fields) align with observed spectral breaks. Future measurements like neutrino flavor ratios or Glashow resonance could distinguish scenarios, and parameters suggest AGN cores as potential sources.
AI.motivation: To determine if a single type of astrophysical source (standard candle-like) primarily contributes to the observed diffuse neutrino flux, especially given the observed spectral break around 30 TeV. This addresses gaps in understanding the origin and mechanisms behind high-energy neutrino production.
AI.method: Employed a SOPHIA-based photo-pion interaction model including thermal target photons and high-energy processes (multi-pion production). Conducted multi-parameter fits to evaluate factors like target photon temperatures (0.1-1 keV), proton energy limits (PeV scale), and magnetic fields (10 kG range) affecting secondary particles. Analyzed scenarios for how these factors shape the neutrino spectrum.
AI.result: Identified that target photon temperatures between 0.1-1 keV optimally fit observational data. Determined three energy-limiting mechanisms (soft injection spectra, PeV proton energy cutoffs, or 10 kG magnetic fields) as viable explanations for spectral breaks. Highlighted AGN cores as potential source candidates based on derived parameters.
AI.conclusion: Future neutrino observations (flavor composition, Glashow resonance) will help validate or refine the proposed single-source model. The model parameters suggest AGN cores as plausible progenitors, emphasizing the need for multi-messenger astrophysics to confirm source identity and underlying physics.

title: Optical Magnus effect on gravitational lensing
summary: The optical Magnus effect refers to transverse shift of a trajectory of light caused by its polarization and appears as a correction to geometrical optics at the linear order in wavelength. Here, we start from Maxwell's equations in a curved spacetime to derive the equation of motion for a wave packet of circularly polarized light, which confirms the known result involving the helicity-dependent anomalous velocity with some generalization and clarification. We then study possible consequences of the optical Magnus effect on gravitational lensing in the Schwarzschild spacetime as well as under a weak gravitational potential in an expanding spacetime. Among others, by formulating the lens equation modified to incorporate the optical Magnus effect, the Einstein ring is found impossible to emerge from a point source for any axially symmetric thin lens. Analytic solutions to the modified lens equation are also obtained for simple lens models, illuminating how image formation is affected by the optical Magnus effect.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the optical Magnus effect's impact on gravitational lensing in curved spacetime, showing that it prevents the formation of an Einstein ring from a point source under axially symmetric thin lenses and provides analytic solutions for modified lens equations.
AI.motivation: To investigate how the optical Magnus effect, a polarization-induced transverse shift in light trajectories, affects gravitational lensing phenomena in different spacetime geometries, extending beyond classical geometrical optics.
AI.method: Deriving equations of motion from Maxwell's equations in curved spacetime, analyzing gravitational lensing scenarios in Schwarzschild spacetime and expanding spacetime with weak gravitational potential, formulating a modified lens equation incorporating the optical Magnus effect.
AI.result: The modified lens equation shows axially symmetric thin lenses cannot produce Einstein rings from point sources. Analytic solutions for simple lens models demonstrate the effect's influence on image formation.
AI.conclusion: The optical Magnus effect introduces measurable deviations in gravitational lensing, challenging classical expectations and offering new observational signatures for testing light-polarization interactions in curved spacetimes.

title: Late-Onset Energy Injection in Type Ic SNe and W-Shaped O II Absorption in SLSNe-I
summary: We show that delayed (weeks-months) energy injection into expanding Type Ic supernova (SN) ejecta can reproduce the luminosity and spectral evolution of hydrogen-poor superluminous SNe (SLSNe-I). Late-time reheating sets the radiation temperature and density needed for the W-shaped OII absorption near peak, explaining its disappearance as the ejecta cools without extra excitation mechanisms. In our model, the neutron star (NS) undergoes a core phase transition to deconfined quark matter at time t_QN, triggering rapid magnetic field amplification and forming a hybrid star (HS; a QCD-magnetar). This Quark-Nova (QN) resets the central engine, weeks to months after the SN, by converting the NS rotational energy into renewed energy injection, producing two powering epochs separated by a delay determined by hadron-to-quark microphysics. The model reproduces photometric and spectroscopic evolution of SLSNe-I such as iPTF13ajg, SN2010gx, PTF09cnd, and PTF09atu. We predict a systematic offset between spectroscopic and photometric ages when pre-QN emission is below detection limits, and discuss observational signatures distinguishing QCD-magnetars from standard magnetars. Double-peaked SLSNe-I may probe the hadron-quark transition, constraining quark-matter parameters like deconfinement density and surface tension.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that delayed energy injection from a Quark-Nova (QN) event, triggered by a neutron star's phase transition to quark matter, explains the superluminous Type Ic supernovae (SLSNe-I) observations, including their luminosity, spectral features, and time-evolving signatures.
AI.motivation: To resolve discrepancies in traditional models of SLSNe-I, particularly the origin of their high luminosity, W-shaped OII absorption features, and the lack of sustained energy sources over months. Existing models like magnetars struggle to account for late-time energy injection and specific spectral evolution.
AI.method: Develops a model where a neutron star undergoes a core phase transition to quark matter (forming a hybrid star), which amplifies its magnetic field and releases stored rotational energy weeks to months post-supernova. This 'Quark-Nova' re-energizes the ejecta, matching observed light curves and spectra of SLSNe-I like iPTF13ajg.
AI.result: The model replicates key observational features of multiple SLSNe-I, including the W-shaped OII absorption near peak brightness and fading without additional excitation. It predicts discrepancies between photometric and spectroscopic ages when early emissions are undetected, and offers testable signatures distinguishing QCD-magnetars from standard magnetars.
AI.conclusion: Quark-Novae provide a viable mechanism for powering SLSNe-I, linking astrophysical observations to dense-matter physics. The hadron-quark transition timing and resulting observational signatures can constrain quark matter parameters, bridging nuclear physics and high-energy astrophysics.

title: Impact of numerical-relativity waveform calibration on parametrized post-Einsteinian tests
summary: Testing general relativity in the strong-field and highly dynamical regime is now possible through current gravitational-wave observations, where even a single high-quality detection can place competitive constraints on deviations from Einstein's theory. The parametrized post-Einsteinian framework provides a theory-agnostic approach to search for such deviations, but it typically assumes that systematic uncertainties in the base waveform model, particularly those arising from calibration to numerical relativity, are negligible. In this work, we investigate how calibration errors in the late-inspiral fitting coefficients of the IMRPhenomD waveform model can lead to spurious detections of departures from general relativity in parametrized tests. We use an uncertainty-aware version of IMRPhenomD, recalibrated to a set of numerical relativity surrogate waveforms and equipped with a probabilistic description of its fitting coefficients, to simulate general-relativity-consistent signals. We inject these signals into an O5 ground-based detector network and recover them with the original IMRPhenomD model augmented with a parametrized post-Einsteinian phase deformation. We find that false violations of general relativity using this model arise for network signal-to-noise ratios as low as 60. When the uncertainty-aware model is used instead, the inferred parametrized post-Einsteinian phase deformation remains consistent with zero even for signals with a signal-to-noise ratio up to 330. These results demonstrate the need to account for numerical relativity calibration uncertainty in order to perform reliable inspiral tests of general relativity. They also illustrate that explicitly incorporating numerical relativity calibration uncertainty into the waveform model preserves our ability to robustly test general relativity.

AI.tldr: The study highlights the importance of accounting for calibration uncertainties in waveform models when testing general relativity with gravitational waves. Using an uncertainty-aware IMRPhenomD model prevents spurious results, showing that calibration errors can lead to false detections of deviations from GR, even at moderate signal strengths.
AI.motivation: To address the assumption that systematic uncertainties in waveform models (specifically calibration errors from numerical relativity) are negligible and to ensure reliable tests of general relativity using gravitational-wave data.
AI.method: The researchers compared analyses using the standard IMRPhenomD model and an uncertainty-aware version. They injected GR-consistent signals into simulated O5 detector data, analyzed them with both models, and evaluated parametrized post-Einsteinian parameters to check for false GR violations.
AI.result: The standard model produced false GR deviations at SNR 60+, while the uncertainty-aware model remained consistent with GR even at SNR 330. This demonstrates that calibration uncertainties significantly affect test outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Calibration uncertainties in waveform models must be explicitly included to avoid misleading results in GR tests. Incorporating these uncertainties preserves the integrity and reliability of gravitational-wave-based relativistic tests.

title: Baryon Asymmetry of the Universe from Preon Confinement and Supersymmetry Breaking
summary: We propose a mechanism for generating the baryon asymmetry of the universe (BAU) within a supersymmetric preon model based on $U(1)\times SU(3)$ internal symmetry. Integrating out massive preons at the confinement scale $Λ_{cr} \sim 10^{14}$ GeV induces a Chern-Simons (CS) term in the effective gauge action via the Callan-Harvey anomaly inflow mechanism, deriving rather than assuming the topological structure required for the model. The confinement transition, at which supersymmetry breaks intrinsically through the differential condensation of fermionic and bosonic preon composites, provides the necessary departure from thermal equilibrium. The time-varying CS coefficient sources a net topological charge, which the anomaly equation converts directly into baryon number. Electroweak sphalerons are out of equilibrium at $Λ_{cr}$, protecting the asymmetry from immediate washout. Matching the observed baryon-to-entropy ratio $η\sim 8.7\times 10^{-10}$ constrains the fermion/boson condensation asymmetry to $ε\simeq 0.022$, a value consistent with a one-loop origin. This mechanism is structural rather than statistical, and the electroweak phase transition plays no role.

AI.tldr: Proposes a supersymmetric preon model with a U(1)×SU(3) symmetry to generate the universe's baryon asymmetry via a Chern-Simons term induced by confinement and anomaly inflow, avoiding statistical processes and requiring specific conditions like out-of-equilibrium sphalerons.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed baryon asymmetry (η ~10^-9) through a mechanism rooted in fundamental particle physics, avoiding assumptions about topological structures and leveraging intrinsic supersymmetry breaking during confinement.
AI.method: Uses a supersymmetric preon model; confinement induces a Chern-Simons term via Callan-Harvey anomaly inflow. Supersymmetry breaks via differential preon composite condensation, creating CP violation. The time-varying CS coefficient generates net topological charge converted to baryon number via anomaly equations.
AI.result: The model successfully matches the observed baryon-to-entropy ratio (η) with a condensation asymmetry ε ~0.022, consistent with loop corrections. Electroweak sphalerons remain out of equilibrium, preventing washout, and the mechanism depends on structure rather than thermal statistics.
AI.conclusion: A novel structural baryogenesis mechanism emerges from preon model dynamics, independent of electroweak phase transitions, providing a theoretically grounded explanation for BAU through topological and symmetry-breaking processes at ~10^14 GeV scales.

title: \texttt{py5vec}: a modular Python package for the 5-vector method to search for continuous gravitational waves
summary: We present \texttt{py5vec}, a Python package for implementing and extending the 5-vector method, used to search for continuous gravitational wave (CW) signals. We also provide a comprehensive theoretical review of the 5-vector method and extend the relative likelihood formalism by marginalizing over the noise variance, resulting in a more robust Student's t-likelihood, and over the initial phase to account for pulsar glitches. \texttt{py5vec} provides a modular architecture that separates data representation, signal demodulation, and statistical inference into independent abstract stages. It supports multiple input data formats and interoperates with existing Python software, providing a bridge between different approaches. For example, using a \texttt{bilby}-based interface, \texttt{py5vec} implements Bayesian parameter estimation within the 5-vector formalism for the first time. The modular design also allows for making exact multi-level and direct comparisons between other software, such as \texttt{cwinpy} and \texttt{SNAG} in MATLAB. In \texttt{py5vec}, we implement a multidetector targeted search for known pulsars, validated using LIGO data from the O4a run and hardware injections, demonstrating consistent reconstruction of signal parameters. This package therefore provides a flexible platform for current targeted searches and for future extensions to other CW search strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces py5vec, a Python package implementing and extending the 5-vector method for continuous gravitational wave (CW) searches. It includes a Student's t-likelihood robustness enhancement, phase marginalization for glitches, modularity for interoperability with tools like bilby, cwinpy, and SNAG, and validation using LIGO O4a data.
AI.motivation: To provide a flexible, modular platform for CW searches that improves upon existing methods by addressing noise variance and phase uncertainties, while enabling interoperability between different software tools and facilitating future search strategy extensions.
AI.method: Developed py5vec with a three-stage architecture (data representation, demodulation, inference), extended the 5-vector method with marginalized Student's t-likelihood and phase over glitches, and integrated with bilby for Bayesian parameter estimation. Validated using LIGO O4a data and hardware injections.
AI.result: Successful parameter reconstruction in simulations, demonstrating the package's reliability. Established a bridge between Python and MATLAB tools, enabling comparative studies between cwinpy, SNAG, and py5vec.
AI.conclusion: py5vec offers a versatile framework for current targeted CW searches and supports future methodological advancements, emphasizing reproducibility and collaborative innovation in gravitational wave astronomy.

title: First detailed optical spectroscopic observations of the supernova remnants G107.7-5.1 and G150.3+4.5
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We present optical spectroscopic observations of the supernova remnants (SNRs) G107.7$-$5.1 and G150.3+4.5, each spanning nearly 3 degree. Both remnants were recently examined in the optical band through a deep H$α$ and [OIII] emission-line imaging survey, which led to the discovery of G107.7$-$5.1. Using long-slit spectra obtained with the 1.5-m Russian-Turkish Telescope (RTT150), we investigate the physical conditions of the pre-shock and post-shock gas in the optical filamentary regions of the SNRs. The SNR nature of G107.7$-$5.1 is confirmed by the measured [SII]/H$α$ and [NII]/H$α$ ratios, which range from 0.56 to 0.86 and from 0.7 to 1.4, respectively. A similar conclusion is reached for G150.3+4.5, where the observed [SII]/H$α$ (0.43-0.92) and [NII]/H$α$ (0.49-1.29) ratios likewise support a shock-excitation origin. Further confirmation of the shock-excited nature of both SNRs comes from their consistency with recent diagnostic diagrams based on multiple emission-line ratios. The [OIII]/H$β$ line ratios measured in both remnants indicate shocks with complete recombination zones and are consistent with shock velocities of $\gtrsim$100 km s$^{-1}$. The electron densities ($n_{\rm e}$), derived from the [SII] $λ$6716/$λ$6731 line ratios, exhibit substantial variation in the spectra of both SNRs. Additionally, extinction variations observed across the remnants suggest the presence of significant dust structures along the line of sight. We conclude that these two remnants display remarkable similarities across multiple diagnostic spectral properties, consistent with previous reports indicating comparable GeV gamma-ray characteristics.

AI.tldr: The paper presents optical spectroscopic analysis of two supernova remnants, G107.7-5.1 and G150.3+4.5, confirming their shock-excited nature through emission-line ratios and diagnostic diagrams. Both show similar properties in shock velocities (~100 km/s), electron densities, and dust extinction variations, aligning with their observed GeV gamma-ray similarities.
AI.motivation: To confirm the supernova remnant status of newly discovered G107.7-5.1 and analyze physical conditions of both SNRs using optical spectroscopy, as well as compare their shock-excitation characteristics.
AI.method: Long-slit optical spectroscopy with RTT150 telescope; measured emission-line ratios ([SII]/Hα, [NII]/Hα, [OIII]/Hβ) to assess shock excitation and electron densities. Used diagnostic diagrams for classification and shock velocity estimates.
AI.result: Confirmed SNR nature via characteristic line ratios; both show shock velocities ~100 km/s, variable electron densities, and dust structures. Spectral properties align with their GeV gamma-ray similarities.
AI.conclusion: The two SNRs exhibit consistent shock-excited features, suggesting similar evolutionary stages or environments. Their optical and gamma-ray similarities imply potential shared astrophysical mechanisms.

title: Quasi-pole quintessential inflation in metric-affine gravity
summary: We study quintessential inflation in the framework of metric-affine gravity. It is well known that non-minimal couplings with the Holst invariant can generate a quasi-pole inflationary behaviour resulting in a Starobinsky-like phenomenology. The same quasi-pole behaviour can also be used in order to "flatten" the scalar potential in the Dark Energy era providing a successful framework for quintessential inflation. Agreement with all the observational constraints, reduces the predicted scalar spectral index to a narrow window: $0.966 \lesssim n_s \lesssim 0.967$, making the model highly testable and falsifiable.

AI.tldr: The paper explores quintessential inflation within metric-affine gravity, using non-minimal Holst invariant couplings to achieve both Starobinsky-like inflation and a flattened scalar potential for dark energy. Observational constraints restrict the scalar spectral index to 0.966-0.967, making the model testable.
AI.motivation: To unify inflation and dark energy within a single framework (quintessential inflation) while addressing observational constraints, particularly achieving a viable scalar spectral index.
AI.method: Studying quintessential inflation in metric-affine gravity with non-minimal couplings to the Holst invariant. Analyzing how quasi-pole inflationary behavior from these couplings generates Starobinsky-like results during inflation and flattens the scalar potential in the dark energy era. Comparing predictions against observational data.
AI.result: The model predicts a narrow scalar spectral index range (0.966 ≤ n_s ≤ 0.967) consistent with observations, enhancing testability and falsifiability.
AI.conclusion: This unified framework successfully bridges inflation and dark energy phases via metric-affine gravity, offering a testable model with precise predictions for cosmic parameters.

title: A Special $E_6\to G(2) \times SU(3)_A$ Embedding for Standard Model and Dark Matter
summary: I developed a grand unified framework based on a special (non-regular) embedding of the exceptional group $E_6$ in which the first stage of symmetry breaking chain realizes $E_6\to G(2) \times SU(3)_A$. The exceptional factor $G(2)$ plays the role of a hidden strong sector, while $SU(3)_A$ acts as a progenitor of the electroweak gauge group. Subsequent breaking steps, $G(2)\to SU(3)_C$ and $SU(3)_A\to SU(2)_L\times U(1)_Y$, recover the Standard Model at low energies while preserving a confining dark gauge sector. Hypercharge is defined from the $t_{8}$ generator of $SU(3)_A$, fixing the electroweak coupling normalization and identifying the Standard Model Higgs doublet within a larger exceptional representation. The special embedding naturally suppresses tree-level leptoquark couplings that typically mediate proton decay in regular grand unified theories. The scalar potential for the three Higgs sectors has been constructed, deriving the heavy gauge-bosons spectrum and presenting a consistent one-loop running of the gauge couplings across the intermediate scales, which is shown to satisfy $E_6$ unification. The $E_6$ $\mathbf{27}$ representation neatly packages one SM family, while exotic states are lifted vectorlike and made ultraheavy. All non-Standard Model Higgs fields and \textit{broken} massive vectors are found to be invisible to colliders searches. The $G(2)$ gluons ensemble confines into heavy dark glueballs without communications with $SU(3)_A$ and $E_6$ sectors. Cosmological history is analyzed in details, including topological defects, inflation and reheating, demonstrating that monopole relics are naturally diluted. The resulting framework provides a minimal unified, self-consistent exceptional apparatus which includes the Standard Model and a dark matter sector which is secluded by the group-theoretic orthogonality.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a grand unified framework based on an exceptional group E6 embedding, resulting in a Standard Model at low energies along with a confining dark sector. It suppresses proton decay, organizes particles in E6 representations, and includes a secluded dark matter sector.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of regular GUTs, such as proton decay mediated by leptoquarks, and unify the Standard Model with a dark sector using exceptional group theory.
AI.method: A non-regular embedding of E6 into symmetry breaking chains E6→G2×SU(3)_A, followed by further breaks to recover SM gauge groups. Hypercharge is defined via the t8 generator of SU(3)_A. Scalar potentials and gauge coupling unification are analyzed at one-loop.
AI.result: Constructs a consistent framework with E6 unification, natural suppression of proton decay, secluded dark matter via G2 confinement, and consistent cosmological history without problematic relics.
AI.conclusion: The E6-based model provides a minimal, self-consistent unification of SM with a dark sector through exceptional group embeddings, addressing key theoretical and experimental issues in grand unification.

title: A 5kHz modulator for pyramid wavefront sensors
comment: 20 pages, 11 Figures. Published in Experimental Astronomy
summary: Despite the emergence of new types of wavefront sensors, the modulated pyramid wavefront sensor remains the workhorse for ELT instrumentation, and is among the options even for advanced high-contrast, high-Strehl instrumentation like PCS and SAXO+. To achieve the required degree of wavefront control, an operation at frequencies of 3kHz, ideally up to 5kHz, is necessary, requiring an optomechanical device capable of delivering accurate circular modulation patterns with these frequencies. Here, we present tests of a novel type of high-frequency modulator based on shearing piezo actuators. The modulator prototype moves a flat circular mirror (15mm diameter) with a tip-tilt range of plus/minus 50 arcsec. At a typical 10mm pupil diameter on the modulator mirror, and operating at 2.2$μ$m, this will create a modulation circle with a radius of slightly greater than 2 $λ$/D. While this is less than conventionally specified for most instruments, it should already be sufficient for any practical application except for very bad conditions or extended targets. We performed modulation tests at frequencies between 250 Hz to 5 kHz using a test setup including a modulated laser beam probed with a high-speed camera. The prototype showed stable behaviour during a one-hour-long operation at a maximum frequency of 5 kHz and with negligible heat generation. The maximum modulation amplitude was 60 arcsec. We observed very accurate reproduction of the input modulation pattern with typical ellipticities less than 1% and random deviations below 0.2% for frequencies below 4.5kHz. These tests demonstrate the prototype's capabilities and could be followed by on-sky tests or the integration of the modulator into XAO testbeds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel high-frequency modulator for pyramid wavefront sensors using piezo actuators, demonstrating stable operation up to 5 kHz with minimal ellipticity and deviations, suitable for Extremely Large Telescopes and advanced instruments.
AI.motivation: The need for high-frequency wavefront control (up to 5 kHz) in Extremely Large Telescopes (ELTs) and advanced instruments like PCS and SAXO+ drives the development of a reliable modulator capable of precise modulation patterns.
AI.method: A prototype modulator with a 15mm mirror driven by piezo actuators was tested using a modulated laser beam and high-speed camera. Tests evaluated performance from 250 Hz to 5 kHz, measuring accuracy, stability, and thermal effects.
AI.result: The prototype operated stably at 5 kHz for an hour with <1% ellipticity and <0.2% deviation at frequencies below 4.5 kHz. It showed negligible heat generation and 60 arcsec maximum modulation amplitude.
AI.conclusion: The modulator meets performance targets for many ELT applications, suggesting potential for on-sky testing or integration into adaptive optics systems like XAO testbeds.

title: Exploring memory-burdened primordial black holes with ultra-high-energy cosmic-rays
summary: Quantum backreaction effects may quench Hawking evaporation through a ``memory burden'', allowing primordial black holes (PBHs) with formation masses well below $10^{15}~\mathrm{g}$ to survive to the present and contribute to the dark matter. We show that ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (UHECRs) provide a powerful and previously unexplored probe of this scenario. We compute the proton and neutron emission from memory-burdened PBHs, including the Galactic-halo contribution and the extragalactic proton component, and confront it with the Pierre Auger Observatory proton spectrum and its EeV neutron limits from the Galactic plane. This yields new constraints on the PBH dark-matter fraction as a function of the PBH formation mass and the evaporation-suppression parameter $k$. For $k\gtrsim 3$ the non-observation of ultra-high-energy protons leads to bounds competitive with those from UHE gamma rays, while neutron limits remain comparable to high-energy neutrino constraints. Our results highlights the key role of multi-messenger astronomy in constraining beyond-the-standard-model scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how quantum backreaction effects might allow primordial black holes (PBHs) with low formation masses to survive and contribute to dark matter, proposing ultra-high-energy cosmic rays as a new probe. It calculates emission spectra from these PBHs and sets constraints using observational data, showing multi-messenger astronomy's importance.
AI.motivation: To investigate if quantum backreaction ('memory burden') enables PBHs with masses below 10^15 g to exist today as dark matter, and to use UHECR observations to test this scenario and constrain PBH parameters.
AI.method: Computed proton/neutron emission from memory-burdened PBHs considering Galactic halo and extragalactic sources. Compared results with Pierre Auger proton spectrum and neutron limits from Galactic plane observations. Analyzed constraints on PBH dark matter fraction vs mass and suppression parameter k.
AI.result: Non-detection of ultra-high-energy protons imposes competitive constraints with gamma-ray bounds for k ≥ 3; neutron limits match neutrino constraints. These provide new parameter space boundaries for PBH dark matter models with evaporation suppression.
AI.conclusion: UHECRs are a powerful untapped tool for probing PBH dark matter scenarios. Multi-messenger approaches combining different astrophysical observations are essential for constraining beyond-standard-model physics like modified black hole evaporation.

title: Non-metricity effects on electron scattering in bumblebee gravity
comment: 29 pages, and 4 figures. Comments/suggestions are welcome
summary: We investigate non-metricity effects on electron scattering in metric-affine bumblebee gravity, where spontaneous Lorentz symmetry breaking is induced by a vector field acquiring a nonzero vacuum expectation value. Treating the affine connection as an independent variable and integrating it out leads to an effective description in which non-metricity modifies the dispersion relation of the bumblebee modes. From the full momentum-space propagator, we determine the pole structure that governs the interaction and construct the corresponding static Green function and interparticle potential. For a purely timelike background, the dispersion relation remains isotropic and produces a Coulomb potential with a uniformly rescaled effective coupling; consequently, the scattering amplitude preserves the Rutherford angular dependence, with the Lorentz-violating parameter entering only as an overall multiplicative factor. In contrast, a spacelike background induces anisotropy in the dispersion relation, leading to an orientation-dependent potential characterized by a quadrupolar modulation. This anisotropic structure propagates to the differential and integrated cross sections, introducing directional dependence while preserving the long-range character of the interaction. Finally, we consider phenomenological constraints from atomic physics. Hydrogen spectroscopy constrains the isotropic sector associated with the timelike configuration, whereas searches for anisotropies provide stronger limits on the quadrupolar contribution governed by $ξb^{2}$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how non-metricity in metric-affine bumblebee gravity, with a vector field breaking Lorentz symmetry, affects electron scattering. Different background vector field orientations (timelike vs spacelike) produce distinct effects: isotropic Coulomb-like potentials with rescaled couplings for timelike, and anisotropic quadrupolar potentials with directional cross sections for spacelike. Constraints from hydrogen spectroscopy and anisotropy searches are analyzed.
AI.motivation: To explore deviations from metric gravity theories by analyzing non-metricity effects on scattering processes in Lorentz-violating bumblebee gravity models, providing insights into possible observable signatures.
AI.method: Derive effective non-metricity-modified dispersion relations via propagator analysis, construct static Green functions and interparticle potentials, and calculate differential/integrated cross sections. Compare timelike vs spacelike vector field configurations
AI.result: Timelike bumblebee background gives isotropic Coulomb potential with modified coupling, unchanged Rutherford scattering. Spacelike case introduces quadrupolar anisotropy in potentials and cross sections, maintaining long-range interaction
AI.conclusion: Non-metricity-induced Lorentz violation leaves characteristic directional signatures in scattering observables. Atomic physics experiments (hydrogen spectroscopy, anisotropy searches) constrain the model parameters effectively, with tighter bounds on spacelike-induced anisotropies.

title: Cosmological Collider Searches beyond the Hubble Scale with Planck Data
summary: Searches for primordial non-Gaussianity (NG) has the potential to not only reveal the physics of cosmic inflation, but also the structure of fundamental interactions at the highest energies. The cosmological collider (CC) physics program exemplifies this possibility and demonstrates how searches for oscillatory NG can lead to mass-spin spectroscopy of extremely heavy states. Adopting an effective field theory approach, we find the class of Feynman diagrams that can give the largest NG mediated by a heavy scalar particle with mass $M\sim H$, the inflationary Hubble scale. We compute the full shape of the NG and perform the first search for this shape using Planck data, finding no evidence for NG. This search loses its sensitivity as $M\gg H$ since quantum vacuum fluctuations cannot efficiently produce such heavier particles. We then focus on a mechanism where a chemical potential excites on-shell scalar particles with mass $M\gg H$. Computing the full shapes, we perform the first CC search for particles parametrically heavier than $H$ using Planck data. For a range of chemical potential $ω$ and $M$ satisfying $ω-M \simeq 3H$, we find a global $1.7σ$ evidence for non-zero NG, after taking into account the look-elsewhere effect.

AI.tldr: The study explores detecting heavy particles using cosmological collider physics via primordial non-Gaussianity (NG) analysis of Planck data, finding a 1.7σ signal for specific particle masses and chemical potentials beyond the inflationary Hubble scale.
AI.motivation: To probe high-energy physics during cosmic inflation and test models involving extremely massive particles by analyzing NG signatures in cosmic microwave background data.
AI.method: Used effective field theory to identify dominant Feynman diagrams for NG from heavy scalars, calculated NG shapes for both M~H and M≫H scenarios, and analyzed Planck data for oscillatory NG patterns.
AI.result: No evidence found for NG with M~H, but a marginal 1.7σ signal detected for M≫H cases when ω-M≈3H, considering the look-elsewhere effect.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates methodological advances in detecting particles far heavier than the Hubble scale via CC physics, though results remain inconclusive and require future higher-sensitivity data.

title: Optimising the FRB Search Pipeline for the Northern Cross Radio Telescope
summary: FRB search pipelines are being developed to operate under strict real-time constraints while maintaining sensitivity to short-duration transient signals. In incoherent dedispersion based pipelines such as Heimdall, apart from observation bandwidth and number of beams, detection performance and computational throughput are strongly dependent on the choice of processing parameters, which are often selected heuristically. In this work, we present a systematic evaluation of key dedispersion and matched filtering parameters and quantify their impact on both detection accuracy and runtime performance. A controlled synthetic injection framework is developed in which artificial FRB pulses with known DMs, SNRs, and pulse widths are embedded into realistic filterbank data containing instrumental noise representative of observations from the Northern Cross radio telescope. Using this framework, a grid of Heimdall configurations is explored, spanning DM tolerance, boxcar filter width, and processing gulp size. Detection performance is assessed by comparing recovered and injected signal properties, while computational performance is evaluated through end-to-end processing time measurements. The results reveal clear trade-offs between sensitivity and throughput across parameter choices. We identify an empirically optimal configuration that provides burst recovery while maintaining processing speeds exceeding real-time requirements. While the specific optimal parameters are derived for the Northern Cross, the methodology and findings are broadly applicable to any real-time transient detection pipeline employing matched-filtering and dedispersion, and are particularly relevant for low-frequency radio telescopes with similar observing configurations. These findings demonstrate the value of data-driven parameter evaluation for improving the performance of real-time transient detection pipelines.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates parameters in FRB search pipelines like Heimdall, using synthetic FRB injections into realistic data from the Northern Cross telescope. Key parameters (DM tolerance, filter width, gulp size) are analyzed for their impact on detection accuracy and speed. An optimal configuration balancing sensitivity and real-time processing is identified, applicable to similar radio telescope pipelines.
AI.motivation: Existing FRB detection pipelines use heuristic parameter choices, leading to suboptimal performance under real-time constraints. A controlled framework is needed to objectively measure how parameters affect sensitivity and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Synthetic FRB signals with known properties are injected into realistic noise data. A grid search explores DM tolerance, boxcar filter width, and gulp size. Detection performance (signal recovery accuracy) and runtime are measured for each configuration.
AI.result: Trade-offs between sensitivity and throughput are quantified. An optimal parameter set achieving both burst recovery and real-time processing speeds is identified specifically for Northern Cross. The methodology is broadly applicable to other matched-filtering pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven parameter optimization is essential for real-time transient detection. The developed framework provides a reproducible approach for tuning pipelines to balance detection performance with computational constraints.

title: A Candidate Open Cluster Pulsar: Timing Analysis of PSR J1922+3745 in NGC 6791
summary: PSR J1922+3745 was recently identified as a radio pulsar toward the old open cluster NGC 6791, raising the prospect of the first pulsar associated with an open cluster. We report FAST follow-up observations that yield a phase-coherent timing solution, a precise position, a measurement of the spin-down rate and the pulsar's polarization properties. PSR J1922+3745 is consistent with an isolated slow pulsar with a characteristic age of 7.8 Myr, comparable to the small population of long-period pulsars found in globular clusters. Motivated by the potential cluster association, we re-process deeper searches of the NGC 6791 field at higher sensitivity but detect no additional pulsars. We also assess whether HI absorption spectroscopy can provide a useful distance constraint and find that such measurements are unlikely to be constraining with currently available sensitivity. Consequently, existing evidence does not yet establish membership in NGC 6791. Further deep searches for additional pulsars with similar dispersion measures in the cluster field will likely be the most direct path to confirming a physical association.

AI.tldr: PSR J1922+3745 may be associated with the open cluster NGC 6791, but confirmation requires further observations.
AI.motivation: To determine if PSR J1922+3745 is truly part of NGC 6791, an old open cluster.
AI.method: FAST follow-up timing, polarization studies, HI absorption analysis, and deep pulsar searches in the cluster field.
AI.result: No additional pulsars found; HI absorption insufficient for distance measurement; current data don't confirm membership.
AI.conclusion: Further deep searches for pulsars with matching dispersion measures are needed to establish physical association.

title: Ringdown waves from hairy black holes
summary: We derive general formulas for quasi-normal mode (QNM) frequencies of hairy black holes by exploiting the QNM--geodesic correspondence. The black hole hair is treated as an anisotropic fluid perturbatively added to the vacuum black holes (Schwarzschild and Kerr black holes). Under this setting, independent of energy conditions, our formulas offer a systematic method to compute quasi-normal mode frequencies for a broad class of hairy black holes.

AI.tldr: This paper presents general formulas for calculating quasi-normal mode frequencies of hairy black holes using the QNM-geodesic correspondence, treating the hair as a perturbative anisotropic fluid added to vacuum black holes.
AI.motivation: To provide a systematic method for computing QNM frequencies for hairy black holes without relying on specific energy conditions.
AI.method: The authors use the QNM--geodesic correspondence and model the black hole's hair as an anisotropic fluid perturbatively added to Schwarzschild/Kerr backgrounds.
AI.result: Derivation of general formulas applicable to a broad class of hairy black holes, independent of energy conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a versatile tool for studying perturbations in hairy black hole spacetimes through this unified framework.

title: Crowdsourcing Gravitational Waves from Superradiant Axions
summary: Black hole superradiance is a powerful probe of ultralight axions. If nature contains a boson with a mass of order $10^{-12}\,$eV, $\textit{mere vacuum fluctuations}$ will lead to its efficient production around spinning stellar mass black holes, forming a gravitational atom that both drains the black hole spin and decays to produce near-monochromatic gravitational waves. Existing superradiance constraints derive primarily from spin measurements of a handful of identified black holes. Here we instead present a detailed study of the population level effect: gravitational waves arising from both the 100 million black holes in the Milky Way and the stochastic signal from axion clouds throughout the universe. We study the impact of a broad range of systematic uncertainties on the black hole properties and compute the projected axion sensitivity for LIGO, as well as the future instruments Einstein Telescope, Cosmic Explorer, and a high-frequency Magnetic Weber Bar. We demonstrate that LIGO can robustly probe axion masses from roughly $10^{-13}\,$eV to $4 \times 10^{-12}\,$eV. If the black hole population extends to masses slightly below $5\,M_{\odot}$ - as hinted for by LIGO inspiral observations - LIGO would approach $10^{-11}\,$eV. Under that same assumption we show that a future high-frequency detector could push considerably higher, potentially beyond $10^{-10}\,$eV in the most optimistic scenarios, reaching towards the lowest masses within the projected sensitivity of axion dark matter searches.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the use of gravitational waves from population-level observations of black holes and their axion clouds to constrain ultralight boson masses, showing LIGO can probe masses between $10^{-13}$ eV and $4×10^{-12}$ eV, with next-gen detectors potentially reaching down to $10^{-11}$ eV or lower.
AI.motivation: To expand beyond existing superradiance studies relying on individual black hole spin measurements and instead analyze population-level gravitational wave signals from massive black hole populations in the Milky Way and universe to better constrain ultralight axion parameters.
AI.method: Simulates gravitational wave signals from ~100 million stellar-mass black holes in the Milky Way and cosmic stochastic backgrounds, incorporates systematic uncertainties in black hole properties, and evaluates detection prospects for current/future detectors (LIGO, Einstein Telescope, Cosmic Explorer, Magnetic Weber Bar).
AI.result: Demonstrates LIGO's capability to probe axion masses from $10^{-13}-4×10^{-12}$ eV; shows potential for future detectors to reach below $1e-11$ eV if black holes exist slightly below $5M_\odot$. High-frequency detectors might push limits to $1e-10$ eV under optimistic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Population-level gravitational wave analyses significantly extend the sensitivity reach for ultralight axions compared to individual black hole studies, making them essential for future fundamental physics experiments with advanced detectors.

title: Explainable machine learning workflows for radio astronomical data processing
summary: Radio astronomy relies heavily on efficient and accurate processing pipelines to deliver science ready data. With the increasing data flow of modern radio telescopes, manual configuration of such data processing pipelines is infeasible. Machine learning (ML) is already emerging as a viable solution for automating data processing pipelines. However, almost all existing ML enabled pipelines are of black-box type, where the decisions made by the automating agents are not easily deciphered by astronomers. In order to improve the explainability of the ML aided data processing pipelines in radio astronomy, we propose the joint use of fuzzy rule based inference and deep learning. We consider one application in radio astronomy, i.e., calibration, to showcase the proposed approach of ML aided decision making using a Takagi-Sugeno-Kang (TSK) fuzzy system. We provide results based on simulations to illustrate the increased explainability of the proposed approach, not compromising on the quality or accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes combining fuzzy rule-based inference with deep learning to enhance the explainability of ML-driven data processing pipelines in radio astronomy, specifically for calibration, while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Modern radio telescopes generate vast data, making manual pipeline configuration impractical. Existing ML methods are black-boxes, lacking transparency, which hinders astronomers' trust and understanding.
AI.method: The authors integrate Takagi-Sugeno-Kang (TSK) fuzzy systems with deep learning to create an explainable ML framework for calibration tasks. TSK fuzzy rules provide interpretable decision-making, while deep learning handles complex data patterns.
AI.result: Simulation results show the proposed approach maintains high accuracy in calibration while offering clearer, more explainable decisions compared to traditional black-box ML models.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid fuzzy-deep learning method successfully improves explainability without sacrificing performance, fostering trust in automated radio astronomy pipelines and suggesting broader applicability in astronomical data processing.

title: HESS J1832$-$085: evidence for a new gamma-ray binary candidate
summary: The Galactic plane survey conducted by the High Energy Stereoscopic System (H.E.S.S.) has revealed numerous teraelectronvolt (TeV) sources, many of which remain unidentified. HESS~J1832$-$085 is a point-like TeV source lacking a confirmed multiwavelength (MWL) counterpart. In this paper, we present evidence that HESS~J1832$-$085 is likely a gamma-ray binary. We aim to investigate the nature of HESS~J1832$-$085 using {\it Fermi}-LAT and X-ray data, complemented by broadband radiative modeling, to assess its classification as a potential gamma-ray binary. We analyzed $\sim$17.3~yr of {\it Fermi}-LAT data between 0.1 and 500~GeV to establish the gigaelectronvolt (GeV) counterpart of HESS~J1832$-$085, including performing spectral, spatial, and periodicity analyses. Archival X-ray observations were examined to search for a counterpart and to characterize its spectrum and potential variability. The broadband emission was interpreted using models commonly applied to gamma-ray binaries. We detect a point-like GeV gamma-ray source spatially consistent with HESS~J1832$-$085, with spectral properties compatible with known gamma-ray binaries. No significant GeV periodic modulation is detected. A potential X-ray counterpart is identified in archival X-ray data, exhibiting a hard, absorbed spectrum and moderate variability. The broadband spectral energy distribution is reproduced by the adopted binary radiative model. Our results indicate that HESS~J1832$-$085 is likely a gamma-ray binary candidate, motivating dedicated MWL follow-up observations to confirm the source nature.

AI.tldr: The analysis of H.E.S.S. and Fermi-LAT data suggests HESS J1832-085 is a gamma-ray binary candidate, supported by multiwavelength evidence and broadband modeling.
AI.motivation: To identify the nature of the unidentified TeV source HESS J1832-085 and determine if it is a gamma-ray binary through multiwavelength observations and modeling.
AI.method: Analyzed 17.3 years of Fermi-LAT data (0.1-500 GeV) for spectral/spatial properties, searched for GeV periodicity, examined X-ray archives for counterparts, and applied broadband radiative models typical of gamma-ray binaries.
AI.result: Detected a point-like GeV source matching HESS J1832-085 with compatible spectra, found a variable hard X-ray counterpart with absorption features, and modeled the SED consistent with gamma-ray binary characteristics.
AI.conclusion: HESS J1832-085 is likely a gamma-ray binary candidate, requiring further multiwavelength follow-up for confirmation.

title: A minimal fractional deformation of Newtonian gravity
summary: We consider a minimal fractional deformation of Newtonian gravity characterized by a single parameter $α$. In the limit $α\to 1$, the theory reduces to standard Newtonian gravity. Previous works showed that the $Λ$CDM cosmology consistently emerges from this framework. Using a single potential, the model reproduces the full sequence of cosmic evolution (from a nonsingular pre--inflationary phase and a stable inflationary attractor to the radiation- and matter-dominated eras and the present accelerated expansion) and accounts for the growth of large-scale structure for $|α-1|\ll1$, in agreement with current observations. Here we show that the same fractional Newtonian model also describes key weak--field tests, including the perihelion precession of Mercury and the gravitational deflection of light, using a unified potential with the same constraint on $α$. These results suggest that the minimal fractional Newtonian framework may provide a unified phenomenological description of gravitational dynamics from Solar-System scales to cosmology. Finally, this fractional cosmological framework may offer new perspectives on problems such as the cosmological constant, the hierarchy of cosmological scales, and the Hubble tension.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a minimal fractional deformation of Newtonian gravity with parameter α, which unifies gravitational dynamics across scales from Solar System tests to cosmology. It reproduces ΛCDM cosmology and key observations while addressing unresolved cosmic issues.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified gravitational framework that explains both local observations like Mercury's perihelion precession and large-scale phenomena such as cosmic expansion and structure formation within a single potential, potentially resolving discrepancies like the Hubble tension.
AI.method: Extends Newtonian gravity with a fractional parameter α; shows for |α−1|≪1, the model accurately describes cosmic evolution stages (pre-inflationary phase, inflation, radiation/matter eras, accelerated expansion) and Solar System tests using the same potential and α constraint.
AI.result: The fractional Newtonian model successfully accounts for Mercury's perihelion precession, light deflection, and cosmic evolution in agreement with observations. It offers a unified description without requiring additional components like dark energy/dark matter.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a phenomenological bridge between Solar System and cosmological scales, suggesting potential insights into cosmic mysteries like the cosmological constant and Hubble tension. Further observational tests of α's value could validate this framework.

title: The photon-energy spectrum in $B\to X_sγ$ to N$^3$LO: light-fermion and large-$N_{\rm c}$ corrections
summary: We calculate the photon-energy spectrum of the inclusive radiative decay $B\to X_sγ$, induced by the electromagnetic dipole operator $O_7$, to next-to-next-to-next-to-leading order and consider the complete corrections for light fermions, for the contributions with two closed massive fermion loops, and for the limit of large QCD colour factors $N_{\rm c}$ in the remaining part. We discuss the total decay rate both without and with a cut on the photon energy. In addition to the on-shell renormalization of the bottom-quark mass, we also consider the kinetic mass and the MSR mass schemes. The latter two lead to an improved perturbative behaviour of the decay rate.

AI.tldr: The paper calculates the photon-energy spectrum for the B→X_sγ decay at NNLO precision, considering various QCD corrections and mass schemes to improve perturbative behavior.
AI.motivation: To achieve higher precision in predicting the photon spectrum in B→X_sγ decays, which is crucial for testing the Standard Model and probing physics beyond it.
AI.method: The authors compute corrections up to next-to-next-to-next-to-leading order (NNNLO) for light fermions, two-loop massive fermion contributions, and large colour factor terms. They compare mass renormalization schemes (on-shell, kinetic, MSR) to stabilize perturbative results.
AI.result: Complete NNNLO corrections are derived for specific contributions, and the MSR mass scheme shows improved convergence compared to the on-shell scheme.
AI.conclusion: The improved precision and stability via the MSR mass scheme enhance the reliability of theoretical predictions for B→X_sγ decays in particle physics analyses.

title: High-Precision Photometry with a scientific CMOS Camera: II On-Sky Testing of the Marana camera at the NGTS facility
comment: 29 pages, 21 figures, Accepted to RAS Techniques and Instruments
summary: Modern scientific CMOS cameras offer very fast readout speeds and low read noise. In this study, we evaluate the performance of the Andor Marana CMOS camera through on-sky testing carried out at the NGTS facility at the ESO Paranal Observatory in Chile. We mount the Marana camera to an NGTS telescope, and conduct photometric observations of bright stars. In particular, we target transit events around eight known bright exoplanet host stars. Simultaneous observations are carried out using an existing Andor iKon-L CCD camera on a neighbouring NGTS telescope. This allows for a direct comparison of the photometric precision between the CMOS and CCD cameras. We find that the Marana CMOS exhibits a similar level of photometric performance to the CCD camera, achieving 500\,ppm at a 30-minute timescale for a T $=10$\,mag star. Although the CCD has a slightly better quantum efficiency over the NGTS filter range (520-890\,nm), we find that the faster readout speed of the CMOS compared to the CCD means that the CMOS camera detects 20\,\% more photons per unit time for a solar-type star in our standard 10\,s exposure time operation mode. This results in the CMOS performing slightly better photometry in the photon-limited regime. We conclude that modern CMOS cameras, such as the Marana, are very well-suited for astronomical time-series photometry applications.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates the Andor Marana CMOS camera's performance against a CCD camera (Andor iKon-L) at the NGTS facility. Results show comparable photometric precision but superior throughput (20% more photons per unit time) due to faster readout speeds, making CMOS suitable for time-series photometry.
AI.motivation: To assess if modern CMOS cameras can match or exceed CCD performance in on-sky astronomical observations, particularly for time-critical applications like exoplanet transit detection.
AI.method: On-sky tests with simultaneous observations of eight bright exoplanet host stars using Marana CMOS and iKon-L CCD cameras. Compared photometric precision, quantum efficiency, and photon collection efficiency over 10s exposures.
AI.result: CMOS achieved similar 500 ppm precision at 30-min timescale as CCD but gathered 20% more photons due to faster readout. CCD had marginal QE advantage (520-890nm), but CMOS excelled in photon-limited regimes.
AI.conclusion: Modern CMOS cameras like Marana are advantageous for time-series photometry due to faster readout and better photon throughput despite slight QE trade-offs.

title: Resonant scattering at the center of the galaxy cluster PKS 0745-191 with XRISM
summary: We report evidence for the resonant scattering effect at the center of the galaxy cluster PKS 0745-191 with XRISM. We analyzed XRISM/Resolve commissioning-phase observations of the distant cluster PKS 0745-191 (z = 0.103) with a 54 ks exposure. The gain drift was corrected using the onboard modulated X-ray source (MXS), and spectra were extracted from all pixels well illuminated by MXS, the core region (four central pixels, about 100 kpc), and the surrounding region. A single-temperature collisional ionization equilibrium (CIE) model fits the full field-of-view spectrum with kT about 6 keV and a turbulent velocity of about 120 km/s. From the core (r < 50 kpc) spectrum, we detect about 22 percent suppression of the Fe XXV He-alpha resonance (w) line relative to the CIE prediction. We performed Monte Carlo simulations to calculate the resonant scattering (RS) effect using radial profiles from Chandra data. The RS-inferred turbulence agrees with that inferred from Resolve line broadening, demonstrating that RS provides an independent and consistent constraint on ICM turbulence. These results highlight the potential of XRISM/Resolve for turbulence studies in galaxy clusters.

AI.tldr: The study uses XRISM observations of the galaxy cluster PKS 0745-191 to detect Fe XXV He-alpha line suppression due to resonant scattering, confirming turbulence inferred from line broadening and showcasing XRISM's capability in studying ICM turbulence.
AI.motivation: To investigate resonant scattering effects as an independent probe of intracluster medium (ICM) turbulence in galaxy clusters using high-resolution X-ray spectroscopy with XRISM.
AI.method: Analyzed 54 ks XRISM/Resolve data of PKS 0745-191, corrected for gain drift, extracted spectra from core (r < 50 kpc) and surrounding regions. Applied CIE models, Monte Carlo simulations with Chandra radial profiles to quantify resonant scattering impact.
AI.result: Detected ~22% suppression of Fe XXV He-alpha resonance line in the core. Turbulence estimates from resonant scattering align with those from line broadening (~120 km/s).
AI.conclusion: Resonant scattering provides a consistent turbulence diagnostic independent of line broadening. XRISM/Resolve enables precise ICM turbulence measurements in galaxy clusters.

title: Ringdown bounds and spectral density limits from GWTC-3
summary: We establish the first observational bounds on causal nonlocal extensions of gravity characterized by retarded Stieltjes-type kernels with positive spectral density rho(mu) >= 0, using two complementary gravitational-wave channels. From a Bayesian ringdown analysis of 17 binary black hole events in the LIGO-Virgo GWTC-3 catalogue, we set an observational ceiling on universal fractional quasi-normal mode deformations of |epsilon_Omega| < 0.05 (90% C.L.), with a cumulative log Bayes factor ln B = -0.46 +/- 0.77. By mapping published GWTC-3 modified dispersion relation bounds together with the GW170817 propagation speed constraint onto the Stieltjes spectral parameter space (mu_char, M0), we exclude a broad class of infrared-extended spectral densities with mu <= 10^{-6} m^{-2}, thereby ruling out non-trivial regions of the nonlocal kernel parameter space for the first time. The theoretically motivated fiducial range mu_char ~ M_*^2 ~ 10^8-10^10 m^{-2} satisfies all current bounds. We further show that sub-millimetre gravity experiments, which already operate at the predicted causal scale l_* ~ 10^{-4} m, provide the most promising path toward a direct test. These results define quantitative benchmarks against which future observations across the gravitational-wave, short-range gravity, and cosmological sectors can be compared.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes the first observational constraints on nonlocal gravity models using gravitational wave data and rules out certain parameter regions, highlighting the potential of sub-millimetre experiments for future tests.
AI.motivation: To explore causal nonlocal extensions of general relativity by analyzing gravitational wave data and setting observational bounds, addressing gaps in testing infrared-modified gravity theories.
AI.method: Combines Bayesian ringdown analysis of 17 binary black hole events (GWTC-3) with existing GW170817 constraints to map bounds on Stieltjes spectral parameters (mu_char, M0), employing parameter space exclusion techniques.
AI.result: Sets a fractional QNM deformation limit |ΔΩ| < 0.05, excludes spectral densities with mu ≤ 1e-6 m⁻², confirms theoretical predictions (mu_char ~ 1e8-1e10 m⁻²) align with observations, and identifies sub-mm experiments as key for direct testing.
AI.conclusion: Current gravitational wave and ground-based experiments constrain nonlocal gravity models, with future multi-messenger observations needed to further probe these theories and validate fundamental physics predictions.

title: $gg \to ZH$ at NLO matched to parton showers with ggxy and POWHEG
summary: We implement the recently-calculated analytic expressions for the next-to-leading order QCD corrections to $gg\to ZH$ in ggxy. This provides a flexible framework for investigating partonic and hadronic cross sections for various top quark mass renormalization schemes. We augment the $Z$ boson with leptonic decays, including spin correlations and off-shell effects, and furthermore provide an interface to POWHEG. This enables simulations with parton showers, performed using Pythia.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a framework for calculating QCD corrections to the gg→ZH process using ggxy, incorporating top quark mass renormalization schemes, Z boson leptonic decays, and integration with POWHEG/Pythia for parton shower simulations.
AI.motivation: To provide a flexible and accurate tool for evaluating gg→ZH production cross sections, accounting for higher-order QCD effects, different top quark mass schemes, and realistic decay effects with parton showers.
AI.method: Implementation of next-to-leading order QCD corrections into ggxy, inclusion of Z boson leptonic decays with spin correlations and off-shell effects, and development of a POWHEG interface for Pythia-based parton showers.
AI.result: A comprehensive computational framework that allows precise calculations of partonic/hadronic cross sections and enables Monte Carlo simulations with shower effects for gg→ZH processes.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves precision in Higgs/ZZ physics studies, offering versatility across renormalization schemes and enabling realistic event simulations with parton showers.

title: LenghuSky-8: An 8-Year All-Sky Cloud Dataset with Star-Aware Masks and Alt-Az Calibration for Segmentation and Nowcasting
summary: Ground-based time-domain observatories require minute-by-minute, site-scale awareness of cloud cover, yet existing all-sky datasets are short, daylight-biased, or lack astrometric calibration. We present LenghuSky-8, an eight-year (2018-2025) all-sky imaging dataset from a premier astronomical site, comprising 429,620 $512 \times 512$ frames with 81.2% night-time coverage, star-aware cloud masks, background masks, and per-pixel altitude-azimuth (Alt-Az) calibration. For robust cloud segmentation across day, night, and lunar phases, we train a linear probe on DINOv3 local features and obtain 93.3% $\pm$ 1.1% overall accuracy on a balanced, manually labeled set of 1,111 images. Using stellar astrometry, we map each pixel to local alt-az coordinates and measure calibration uncertainties of approximately 0.37 deg at zenith and approximately 1.34 deg at 30 deg altitude, sufficient for integration with telescope schedulers. Beyond segmentation, we introduce a short-horizon nowcasting benchmark over per-pixel three-class logits (sky/cloud/contamination) with four baselines: persistence (copying the last frame), optical flow, ConvLSTM, and VideoGPT. ConvLSTM performs best but yields only limited gains over persistence, underscoring the difficulty of near-term cloud evolution. We release the dataset, calibrations, and an open-source toolkit for loading, evaluation, and scheduler-ready alt-az maps to boost research in segmentation, nowcasting, and autonomous observatory operations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LenghuSky-8, an eight-year all-sky imaging dataset from an astronomical site, providing night-time cloud cover data with high accuracy and astrometric calibration. They use DINOv3 for cloud segmentation (93.3% accuracy) and propose a nowcasting benchmark for cloud motion prediction, showing the challenge of forecasting short-term cloud evolution.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets for ground-based observatories lack long-term night-time coverage, astrometric calibration, or balanced data across day/night phases. This dataset and tools aim to improve cloud monitoring, telescope scheduling, and autonomous observatory operations.
AI.method: They employ DINOv3 with a linear probe for cloud segmentation, achieving high accuracy. Astrometric calibration maps pixels to altitude-azimuth coordinates. A nowcasting benchmark uses ConvLSTM, VideoGPT, etc., to predict cloud evolution, though results show limited improvement over persistence baselines.
AI.result: 93.3% cloud segmentation accuracy on balanced data. Calibration uncertainties of 0.37° (zenith) and 1.34° (30° altitude). Nowcasting experiments show ConvLSTM slightly outperforms persistence. Dataset includes 429,620 frames with 81.2% night coverage.
AI.conclusion: LenghuSky-8 enables robust cloud monitoring and nowcasting, critical for autonomous telescopes. Challenges remain in short-term cloud forecasting, highlighting the need for better models. Open-source tools foster research in observatory automation and sky condition analysis.

title: Time-Lag properties associated with LFQPO in X-ray variability classes of GRS 1915+105: Findings from AstroSat
comment: 15 pages, 6 Figures, Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We present a comprehensive analysis of Low Frequency Quasi-periodic Oscillation (LFQPO) associated time-lags in the persistently variable black hole binary GRS 1915+105 using 441 ks of \textit{AstroSat} observations from March 2016 to March 2019. LFQPO frequency ($1.38-7.38$ Hz) are detected across the $θ$, $β$, $ρ$, and $χ$ classes, with the $χ$ class further subdivided into $χ_1$, $χ_2$, $χ_3$, and $χ_4$ based on spectro-temporal characteristics. Class transitions occur on timescales of a few hours, appearing either as a simultaneous increase in X-ray count rate and QPO frequency, or vice versa, indicating rapid changes in the accretion flow geometry. The $\text{rms}_{\rm QPO}$ increases with QPO frequency up to $\sim 3.4$ Hz and declines at higher frequencies, a trend similar to \textit{RXTE} observations, where peak occurred at $\sim 2$ Hz. Spectro-temporal correlations reveal that increasing $F_{\rm Comp}$ drives higher $\text{rms}_{\rm QPO}$ and decreases the soft-lag magnitude, while $ν_{\rm QPO}$ and $Γ$ also decline, suggesting that the observed time lag may result from the combined effects of multiple physical mechanisms. The consistent increase of $\text{rms}_{\rm QPO}$ with $F_{\rm Comp}$ provides clear evidence that modulated Comptonized photons enhance the rms power ($\text{rms}_{\rm QPO}$). Moreover, the soft-lag ($1.59-13.49$ ms) observed across all QPO frequencies, without the sign reversal at $\sim$ 2 Hz observed in \textit{RXTE} observations, is interpreted within the framework of a dynamical accretion disk model around the black hole.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes LFQPO time-lags in the black hole binary GRS 1915+105 using AstroSat data, revealing correlations between QPO properties and accretion flow changes. It finds that RMS QPO increases with QPO frequency up to ~3.4 Hz and declines afterward, Comptonized photons modulate RMS, and soft-lags persist without sign reversal, indicative of a dynamical disk model.
AI.motivation: To investigate how LFQPO properties (frequency, rms, time-lag) relate to accretion flow dynamics in black hole binaries, extending previous RXTE findings and exploring spectral-temporal dependencies.
AI.method: Performed 441 ks of AstroSat observations spanning 2016-2019, analyzing LFQPO frequencies across four source classes (θ, β, ρ, χ subdivided into χ1-4). Explored correlations between QPO frequency, RMS, time-lags, and spectral parameters (F_Comp, Γ) using time-resolved spectroscopy and timing analysis.
AI.result: Observed class transitions occur within hours linked to accretion geometry changes. RMS_QPO peaks at ~3.4 Hz, declining at higher frequencies. Soft-lags (1.59-13.49 ms) lack sign reversal seen in RXTE. Correlated F_Comp increases with RMS_QPO and reduced soft-lags. QPO frequency and Γ decrease with F_Comp.
AI.conclusion: Comptonization modulates RMS_QPO, dynamical accretion disk explains persistent soft-lags. Observed trends suggest multiple physical mechanisms (geometry changes, Compton scattering, disk dynamics) drive LFQPO behavior.

title: Repetitive Penrose process in Konoplya-Zhidenko rotating non-Kerr black holes
summary: This paper investigates the repetitive Penrose process in Konoplya-Zhidenko rotating non-Kerr black hole, exploring the influence of the deformation parameter on the repetitive Penrose process. After a brief review of the Konoplya-Zhidenko rotating non-Kerr black hole, we study the fundamental equations of the Penrose process in this spacetime, examine the iterative stopping conditions required for the repetitive Penrose process, and obtain the corresponding numerical results. It is concluded that, in addition to previously observed phenomena, under the same decay radius, a larger initial dimensionless deformation parameter $\hatη$ leads to greater values of the energy return on investment and energy utilization efficiency, particularly at higher decay radii. Furthermore, a smaller initial $\hatη$ results in a larger maximum value of the energy return on investment. For energy utilization efficiency, the initial $\hatη$ should take an intermediate value to maximize its peak. Additionally, we find that a larger initial $\hatη$ corresponds to a smaller maximum value of the extracted energy.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how the deformation parameter in Konoplya-Zhidenko non-Kerr black holes affects the efficiency and energy extraction of repetitive Penrose processes, finding optimal conditions based on parameter values and decay radii.
AI.motivation: To understand the impact of black hole geometry (via the deformation parameter) on energy extraction mechanisms, specifically the Penrose process, and to identify conditions maximizing energy efficiency and returns.
AI.method: Analyzed the Penrose process equations in Konoplya-Zhidenko spacetime, derived iterative stopping conditions for repetitive processes, and performed numerical simulations varying the deformation parameter and decay radius.
AI.result: Larger initial deformation parameter (η̂) increases energy return on investment and efficiency at higher decay radii but reduces maximum extractable energy. Smaller η̂ boosts peak return on investment, while intermediate η̂ maximizes efficiency.
