AI.result: All AHW models outperform the original HW model in fitting light baryon masses, producing approximately/asymptotically linear Regge trajectories (M² vs L). AHW₃ additionally provides asymptotic linearity in trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Anomalous dimensions significantly enhance holographic baryon modeling. The power-law AHW₃ model shows promise for future extensions to heavy baryons and QCD studies due to its accuracy and linear Regge asymptotics.

title: Prospects for precision CE$ν$NS measurements with electron-capture neutrinos and lithium-based bolometers
summary: We evaluate the feasibility of high-precision coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering measurements exploiting mono-energetic neutrinos produced by electron-capture (EC) decays of intense radioactive sources, such as $^{51}$Cr or $^{37}$Ar. To fully exploit the high neutrino flux achievable with EC sources, and accounting for the low energy of EC neutrinos, we evaluate the sensitivity of a compact array of bolometric detectors with absorbers based on light elements, such as lithium, oxygen and fluorine. With 1 kg of LiF detectors, source activities of $\sim10^{17}$ Bq, and assuming an energy threshold of 20 eV, we expect to achieve a $\sim 3\%$ precision on the determination of the neutrino flux with 90 days of measurement. Such a measurement would represent a test of the gallium neutrino anomaly, and could disentangle nuclear effects on gallium from the misevaluation of the EC source activity or short-baseline neutrino oscillations.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the feasibility of using mono-energetic neutrinos from electron-capture decays to perform high-precision measurements of coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering (CEvNS) with compact bolometric detectors. It proposes a setup using LiF detectors to achieve ~3% precision on neutrino flux determination, addressing the gallium neutrino anomaly and disentangling nuclear effects from other potential causes.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to test the gallium neutrino anomaly and resolve ambiguities in its origin, such as nuclear effects in gallium, underestimation of source activity, or short-baseline neutrino oscillations.
AI.method: The study uses a compact array of bolometric detectors with light element absorbers (Li, O, F) to detect CEvNS events from mono-energetic neutrinos produced by EC decays (e.g., ⁵¹Cr or ³⁷Ar). Key parameters include 1 kg LiF detectors, ~1e17 Bq source activity, and a 20 eV energy threshold.
AI.result: With the proposed setup, simulations show a ~3% precision on neutrino flux determination within 90 days, enabling sensitivity to the gallium anomaly and potential to discriminate between possible explanations for the anomaly.
AI.conclusion: The proposed experiment provides a viable method to precisely measure neutrino flux and could resolve the gallium neutrino anomaly by distinguishing nuclear effects from source activity mismodeling or oscillations.

title: Two-loop Six-point Planar Massless Feynman Integrals to Higher $ε$ Orders
summary: In this work, we calculate two-loop six-point planar massless Feynman integrals at higher orders in the dimensional regulator $ε$, corresponding to higher transcendental weights. In previous works, these integrals were calculated up to weight four for the purpose of two-loop gauge theory amplitudes. Using modern rational reconstruction methods, we identify the complete alphabet with $269$ letters relevant for the all-weight orders, derive the analytic canonical differential equation and obtain the symbols up to weight six. As a proof of concept, using a new method with Chebyshev pseudospectral transport, we show that the corresponding pure basis can be efficiently evaluated up to weight six, i.e., to $ \mathcal{O}(ε^2)$ in a physical scattering region. The results of this work can be applied to future three-loop amplitudes and provide new data for the formal study of symbols and cluster algebra.

AI.tldr: The authors compute two-loop six-point planar massless Feynman integrals to higher transcendental weights (up to weight six) using advanced rational reconstruction and Chebyshev pseudospectral methods. They derive canonical differential equations, symbols, and validate efficient numerical evaluation.
AI.motivation: Extend previous calculations of two-loop gauge theory amplitudes by computing Feynman integrals beyond weight four, which is essential for higher precision in scattering amplitude computations and theoretical exploration of symbols/cluster algebras.
AI.method: Modern rational reconstruction techniques to identify a 269-letter alphabet; analytic derivation of canonical differential equations; symbol computation up to weight six; numerical evaluation via Chebyshev pseudospectral transport in a physical scattering region.
AI.result: Complete alphabet and symbols up to weight six, validated numerical evaluation of pure basis integrals at O(ε²), and results applicable to future three-loop amplitudes and formal studies.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates capability to handle higher transcendental weight integrals efficiently, opens doors for precision physics applications and mathematical studies of scattering amplitudes.

title: New benchmarks for direct detection of freeze-in dark matter in vector portal models
summary: We investigate the freeze-in of MeV-scale fermionic dark matter (DM) that couples to the Standard Model via a new vector mediator to assess the potential that future direct detection experiments have to observe new physics in either the DM or neutrino sectors. We study the minimal kinetic mixing dark photon of a secluded $U(1)_D$ as well as gauge bosons of the anomaly-free $U(1)_{L_i-L_j}$, with $i,j=e,μ,τ$, and $U(1)_{B-L}$ gauge extensions, exploring the impact of low reheating temperatures on the DM production rates. For the ultralight dark photon scenario, we show that current experimental constraints from electron recoil data in DAMIC-M and PandaX-4T can be avoided if the DM fermion is only a subcomponent (smaller than 40%) of the total cold DM and that future detectors can be sensitive to a DM fraction below 1% for masses above 1 MeV. For a massive dark photon, there are allowed regions of the parameter space with masses in the range 50 MeV $\lesssim m_{\rm DM}\lesssim$ 500 MeV that can be within the reach of direct detection experiments through nuclear recoils if freeze-in occurred at a low reheating temperature. Finally, the case of $U(1)_{L_i-L_j}$ and $U(1)_{B-L}$ is particularly interesting since the discovery of new physics can come from either the DM or the neutrino sector, which features new interactions. We find that freeze-in at low reheating temperatures can reproduce the observed abundance in large parts of the parameter space up to gauge couplings of $g_X\sim10^{-2}$ for MeV DM. Most notably, direct detection experiments will be sensitive to considerable parts of this parameter space in nuclear recoils for 50 MeV $\lesssim m_{\rm DM}\lesssim$ 500 MeV. Additionally, the enhanced signal from solar neutrino coherent scattering is observable in these scenarios, which can serve as a further handle to identify the underlying particle physics model.

AI.tldr: The paper examines MeV-scale fermionic dark matter via vector mediators (dark photon and $U(1)_{L_i-L_j/B-L}$ gauge bosons), showing future detectors can probe DM fractions down to <1% and masses 50-500 MeV through nuclear recoils, especially with low reheating temperatures. Solar neutrino signals may also help identify the model.
AI.motivation: Explore direct detection potential for freeze-in DM in scenarios involving new vector mediators and low reheating temperatures, considering both DM and neutrino sectors for new physics discovery.
AI.method: Analyzed freeze-in mechanisms for MeV DM with dark photon and $U(1)_{L_i-L_j/B-L}$ mediators, calculated production rates under low reheating temperatures. Evaluated constraints from DAMIC-M/PandaX-4T and projected sensitivities of upcoming detectors for electron/nuclear recoils and solar neutrino scattering.
AI.result: Current experiments avoid constraints if DM is <40% subcomponent. Future detectors reach 1% DM fraction for m>1 MeV. Allowed regions exist for 50-500 MeV DM via nuclear recoils. Neutrino sectors offer complementary signals via new interactions. Solar neutrino coherent scattering offers additional model identification.
AI.conclusion: Direct detection experiments are crucial for probing MeV DM and new physics in both DM/neutrino sectors, particularly with low reheating scenarios and vector mediators up to g_X ~1e-2.
